review-radar

A production sentiment system that reads every customer review the moment it lands, separates the angry from the urgent, and puts a human in front of the customer before they churn.

PRODUCTION ML · NLP · MLOpsgithub.com/Dishantpaudel/review-radar
The Problem

Churn signals arrive as text — and nobody reads them

  • The platform receives ~300,000 reviews / month.
  • Human review does not scale. Complaints surface weeks late — after the customer already left.
  • Each angry review is a churn signal with a timer on it.
  • The window to save a customer is hours, not weeks.

The cost of doing nothing: every unanswered complaint is a customer walking out, plus a public review that deters the next buyer.

300k
reviews per month
~95%
of consumers read reviews before buying
0
read in real time today
<2ms
our decision time
PROBLEM
Why Machine Learning at all?

Because the alternatives break at scale

❌ Humans read them

300k reviews × 30s each = 2,500 hours/month. That's ~15 full-time staff, and they'd still be days behind.

❌ Keyword blacklist

"bad" fires on "not bad at all!". Misses sarcasm, misses phrasing nobody predicted. Endless manual upkeep.

✅ Learn from data

The model reads 25,000 labeled examples and derives the patterns itself — including ones no human would list.

The honest nuance: we still use rules — but only where rules are better. Sentiment is learned (ML); urgency is rule-based (transparent, auditable). Right tool, right job.

MOTIVATION
The Machine Learning Lifecycle

This is not a script — it's a pipeline

1Collect data
2Clean
3Feature engineering
4Train
5Evaluate
6Track in MLflow
7Select best model
8Save artifact
9Deploy API
10Monitor + retrain

Every stage has a file

data.pytrain.pyevaluate.pyexperiments.pythreshold.pyurgency.pypredict.pyapp.py

Why the loop matters

Stage 10 feeds back into stage 1. Real reviews drift — new slang, new complaints, new products. A model is a perishable good, so retraining is designed in, not bolted on.

MLOps LIFECYCLE
System Architecture

How a request actually flows

ARCHITECTURE
Step 1 · The Data

IMDB Large Movie Review Dataset

  • Stanford, Maas et al. 2011 — the field's standard sentiment benchmark.
  • 50,000 real reviews, labeled positive / negative.
  • 25k train / 25k test, perfectly balanced 50/50.
  • Only strong opinions kept (≥7★ or ≤4★) → clean labels.
from datasets import load_dataset load_dataset("stanfordnlp/imdb") # src/data.py

Why the train/test split is sacred

The model never sees the 25k test reviews during training. Grading on studied data would be an exam with the answers leaked. Every number in this deck comes from unseen data.

Known limitation

IMDB is a stand-in: movie reviews, balanced 50/50. Real platform reviews are ~80% positive and contain billing complaints the model never saw. This has measurable consequences — slide 16. Production plan: retrain monthly on first-party reviews.

DATA
Step 2–3 · Preprocessing & Feature Engineering

Turning language into mathematics

Raw text"This Movie Was a TOTAL waste of time!!!"
→
Tokenizelowercase, strip punctuation,
split into words
→
TF-IDFword → number
rare + telling = high
→
Vector50,000 numbers
per review

Why TF-IDF, in one table

WordAppears inScore
the, movie, wasalmost every review≈ 0.00
wasterare, opinionated0.61

Filler gets crushed automatically. Nobody writes the word list by hand.

Bigrams earn their place

We keep word pairs, not just single words — because "not good" flips "good".

Proof from our trained model — two of its strongest features are pairs:
"the worst" −5.74 "the best" +3.89

FEATURE ENGINEERING
Step 4 · Model Selection

Why this model, and not the others?

CandidateWhy we did / didn't pick itVerdict
Logistic RegressionLinear models dominate high-dimensional sparse text. Convex → one global optimum. Outputs calibrated probabilities (essential: our threshold math depends on them). Weights are readable.SHIPPED
Random ForestTrees split one feature at a time — hopeless across 50,000 sparse dimensions where signal is spread thin. Slower, bigger, and probabilities are poorly calibrated.rejected
Linear SVMComparable accuracy — genuinely close. But outputs a margin, not a probability. We'd need Platt scaling to recover one. Extra step, no gain.rejected
DistilBERT (pretrained)Reads context, understands negation. Tested it properly — tied our baseline (0.898 vs 0.903) at ~100× the inference cost.benchmarked
Fine-tuned BERTWould likely reach ~0.93. Needs a GPU and hours of training for +3 points we don't need — the gate is 0.85.out of scope

The industrial answer: accuracy stopped being the binding constraint once the gate was cleared. The real constraints are latency, cost, and explainability — and the simple model wins all three. Reaching for the largest available model is a reflex; measuring that the cheapest sufficient one suffices is a decision.

MODEL SELECTION
Step 4 · Inside the Model

Weights it actually learned

Logistic Regression learns one weight per feature. Scoring = add the weights of the words present, squash into 0→1.

# the whole model, conceptually z = intercept + Σ (tfidf[word] × weight[word]) P(negative) = 1 − sigmoid(z)

Real numbers pulled from models/tfidf_logreg.pkl — 100,000 learned features (50k word + 50k character).

Strongest "negative" features

bad −4.11worst −4.05"the worst" −3.34 awful −2.96waste −2.83no −2.75 boring −2.68poor −2.42

Strongest "positive" features

great +3.11"the best" +2.55best +2.30 fun +2.26excellent +2.21"this is" +2.07 perfect +1.93

This is explainability. Any flagged review can be traced to the exact words that drove it. Try asking a 66M-parameter transformer for that.

MODEL INTERNALS
Step 5 · Evaluation — on 25,000 unseen reviews

Measuring what the business cares about

0.903
F1 (negative)
gate ≥ 0.85 ✓
0.896
Recall
angry reviews caught
0.904
Precision
flags that are correct
1.00
Precision@1000
top of the queue

Why NOT accuracy

In production ~80% of reviews are positive. A model that answers "positive" every single time scores 80% accuracy — and catches zero angry customers. Accuracy rewards the useless model. So we measure the negative class only.

Why precision@k is the real KPI

Support has finite hours; they work the queue top-down. Precision@1000 = 1.00 means the first thousand tickets are all genuinely angry. Zero wasted agent time where it counts.

EVALUATION
Step 6 · MLflow — what makes this industrial

Every run is recorded, forever

Without tracking, ML is folklore: "I think the good one had C=1... maybe?" MLflow replaces memory with evidence.

MLflow recordsOurs
Parameters — the settingsmax_features=50000, C=1.0, ngram=1-2
Metrics — the scoresf1_negative=0.9029
Artifacts — the outputstfidf_logreg.pkl
Run ID — the fingerprint3b3c9a50…
# src/train.py — 3 lines buy you reproducibility mlflow.log_params({"C": C, "max_features": max_features}) mlflow.log_metrics(metrics) mlflow.log_artifact(MODEL_PATH)

What it buys us

  • Reproducibility — rebuild any model from its run ID, months later.
  • Comparison — sort 5 experiments by F1, pick the winner with data.
  • Auditability — "why is this model in production?" has a written answer.
  • No lost work — every experiment survives, even the failures.

Browsable at mlflow ui --backend-store-uri sqlite:///mlflow.db

MLflow · TRACKING
Step 6–7 · MLflow in action — real runs from this project

Six experiments → compare → select the winner

mlflow ui · experiment: review-radar: model sweep · sorted by f1_negative ▼
RunfeatureswordsCF1 (neg)Recall
b56a8941 F SHIPPEDword+char50,0001.00.90290.9009typo-robust
139d7b37 Ebigrams50,00010.00.90250.9029best recall
1ba7ce6b Cbigrams50,0001.00.90030.8958
779b273c Bunigrams50,0001.00.88940.8878
ccd167c3 Aunigrams3,0001.00.88350.8786
3e7dbad8 Dbigrams50,0000.10.86860.8562

What the sweep actually found

3k → 50k vocabulary+0.006
adding bigrams+0.011 ← the real win
C = 0.1 (over-regularized)−0.032
adding char n-grams (F)+0.003

Why F ships despite a photo finish

The top three — F, E, C — sit within 0.003 F1. F "wins" by 0.0004 over E: ten reviews out of 25,000, a coin flip, not a better model. So F is not shipped for topping the table. It ships because it is the only one that survives a misspelling — "its borst" — and this clean IMDB test set has almost none to measure that on. The sweep's reliable job was rejecting the losers (D under-fits, A/B lack bigrams) and confirming bigrams as the one real gain.

MLflow · MODEL SELECTION
Benchmark · The Modern Alternative

We tested the transformer properly

  • DistilBERT (SST-2) — a distilled BERT, same family as the models behind modern chatbots.
  • Used pretrained, no fine-tuning → runs on CPU, no GPU bill.
  • Scored 3,000 random test reviews, identical metrics, logged to MLflow.

Why bother, if we ship the simple one?

Because "we didn't try it" is not an answer. Now we can say: we measured it, and the gain didn't justify the cost. That's the difference between a preference and a decision.

What DistilBERT does differently

It reads words in context — it knows "not good" is negative, and handles phrasing it never literally saw. Our baseline approximates this with bigrams.

The catch: 66 million parameters vs our ~100k weights. ~100× slower per review, and it cannot tell you why it decided.

0.898
DistilBERT F1 — vs baseline 0.903
BENCHMARK
The Engineering Decision

Ship the simple model — on purpose

ModelF1 (neg)RecallPrecisionLatencyCostExplainable
Constant guess0.50—————
TF-IDF + LogReg SHIPPED0.9030.9010.905~3 msCPU, ~0Yes ✓
DistilBERT pretrained0.8980.9130.885~100× slowerhighNo
Fine-tuned BERT (est.)~0.93——slowGPU hoursNo

Latency

300k reviews/month in real time. 3 ms scales on one CPU box. 200 ms doesn't.

Inference cost

3.6M predictions/year. At transformer prices that's a GPU fleet for −0.002 F1.

Explainability

Support asks "why was this escalated?" We answer with words. Compliance likes that.

DECISION
Design Feedback #1 · Answered in code

"Negative" and "urgent" are different questions

Most negative reviews are constructive criticism — valuable, but not an emergency. Routing all of them to support drowns the team and buries the real fires.

A second scorer, deliberately rule-based

SignalExamplesWeight
Churn intentcancel · refund · never again0.45
Moneycharged · billing · overcharged0.30
Angerscam · unacceptable · fraud0.15
Shouting!!! · ALL-CAPS ratio0.10

Routing matrix

ConditionRoute
Urgent, not clearly positivesupport — now
Negative, calmfeedback backlog
Neitheranalytics

Urgency escalates on its own. Sentiment is only a floor, so a churn word inside praise ("I'd never cancel, I love it") can't page an agent. Slide 16 explains why that ordering matters.

Why rules, not ML, here?

A support lead must see exactly why a ticket jumped the queue. Our scorer returns the literal matched words. A black box saying "trust me, it's urgent" is unusable in an operational workflow — and impossible to appeal.

DESIGN FEEDBACK #1
Engineering Rigor · what manual testing found

Two real defects — and why they happened

Defect 1 — the lexicon was too literal

InputUrgencyRoute
"I want to cancel my subscription"0.45support
"I dont want my subscription"0.075backlog

Same meaning, opposite outcome — only because cancel was in the word list.

Fix: regex patterns for compositional churn intent, anchored to subscription/account nouns so "I dont want to spoil the ending" can't false-positive.

Defect 2 — the architecture was inverted

# original — sentiment checked FIRST if p_negative < 0.5: return "analytics" # ← vetoes everything
"Charged twice, REFUND, cancelling today!!!"
P(negative) = 0.141 · urgency = 0.850 → analytics

A churning customer was routed to analytics — silently ignored. The movie-trained model is out of domain on billing language, and it was allowed to overrule the purpose-built churn engine.

The principle: an unreliable component must never be able to veto a reliable one. Urgency escalates independently; the clearly-positive floor was later dropped to 0.1 when a third defect showed it was vetoing real churn (see how-it-works.html). Every defect is pinned by a regression test named after the failure — tests went 10 → 23 → 56. Not one was caught by the suite; all were found by using the system. That's why manual testing still earns its place.

ENGINEERING RIGOR
Design Feedback #2 · Answered in code

The threshold is a business decision, not an ML one

The cut-off for "act on this" defaulted to 0.5 — an arbitrary convention. The economics say otherwise:

break-even precision = C / (r · ARPU · M)
= $1 / (0.15 × $10 × 6) = $1/$9 ≈ 0.11
  • Contact costs $1. A save is worth $9.
  • We profit even if 89% of outreach is "wasted."
  • ⇒ the model should flag more, not fewer.

What the sweep found src/threshold.py

Break-even precision0.11
Default threshold0.50
Profit-optimal threshold0.43
Precision at optimum0.88
Safety margin vs break-even8×
Contacted / month159,948

Optimum landed below 0.5 — exactly the predicted direction. Re-run the sweep whenever costs change and the threshold updates itself.

DESIGN FEEDBACK #2
Design Feedback #2 · The evidence

Profit vs threshold

  • Flag more → catch more at-risk customers → more profit, until precision approaches the 11% floor.
  • The peak sits left of 0.5.

Scope of the claim

On this model the gain over t=0.5 is modest (~$1.8k/mo), because it is well-calibrated and rarely emits mid-range scores where the threshold would bite. The gap widens sharply with noisier or imbalanced production data. The reusable method is the deliverable — not one number.

DESIGN FEEDBACK #2
Live Demo · running in this browser, right now

Type a review. Watch it get routed.

Words driving the sentiment score
—
P(negative) — the ML model0.00
Urgency — the rule engine0.00
Urgency signals that fired
—
—

Real logistic-regression inference in JavaScript — a compact word + character n-gram model exported from the pipeline (98.7% verdict agreement with the shipped 50k model), so misspellings like "borst" score correctly here too. The urgency engine is a full port of src/urgency.py — negation, fuzzy matching, the collision guard and the 0.1 floor. On out-of-domain billing text the compact model under-scores sentiment more than the shipped one; the live API is exact.

LIVE DEMO
Step 8–9 · Deployment

The model behind an API

A model in a notebook helps nobody. FastAPI turns it into a service the platform can call on every review event.

# the platform sends POST /review { "text": "Charged twice, cancelling!!!" } # answer in ~3 ms { "p_negative": 0.3463, # unsure — out of domain "urgency": 0.800, # churn is unmistakable "urgency_signals": { "churn_phrases": ["cancel","cancelling"], "money_phrases": ["charged"] }, "route": "support_urgent", "latency_ms": 3.21 }

Production details that matter

  • Model loads once at startup (lifespan hook), not per request — that's the 3 ms.
  • Typed contract via Pydantic — malformed input rejected at the door.
  • /health endpoint — load balancers need a heartbeat.
  • Latency returned in every response — you cannot monitor what you don't measure.

Served by uvicorn service.app:app --port 3000
An interactive OpenAPI console is generated at 127.0.0.1:3000/docs.

CODE EVIDENCE
Step 10 · Business Impact

What the system is worth

profit = saved × (r·ARPU·M) − contacted × C
Reviews / month300,000
Negative share20%
At-risk share of those30%
Win-back rate15%
Value per save (ARPU × 6mo)$60
Contact cost$1
Profit at optimal threshold~$216k / mo
≈ $2.6M
estimated annual effect at the profit-optimal threshold

Scope: this is a model of the business, not a bank statement — it rests on assumed win-back and at-risk rates. The defensible claim is the margin: break-even precision is 11%, the system operates at 88%. An 8× margin means the programme profits even if those assumptions are badly wrong.

BUSINESS IMPACT
Summary

The seven questions this project answers

What problem?

Detect negative and urgent reviews in real time so support acts before the customer churns.

Why ML?

300k reviews/month can't be read by hand; keyword rules break on negation and sarcasm.

Why this model?

TF-IDF + LogReg balances accuracy (F1 0.90), latency (3 ms), cost (CPU) and explainability.

Why compare DistilBERT?

To test whether a transformer earns its cost. It didn't — tie at ~100× the price.

What does MLflow do?

Tracks params, metrics, artifacts and run IDs across 9 runs in 3 experiments → reproducible, evidence-based selection.

How does it work end to end?

Data → preprocess → train → evaluate → MLflow → persist → FastAPI → two scorers → business routing.

What makes it production-oriented?

Automated tests, experiment tracking, a deployed API, business-driven thresholds, a reproducible pipeline, and a documented retraining plan.

Q&A
Wrap-up

Roadmap & takeaways

Shipped

  • Baseline model, F1 0.90 — gate cleared
  • Transformer benchmark + 6-run MLflow sweep
  • Urgency engine + business router
  • Profit-optimal threshold
  • Live API + browser demo, 56 tests, 4 notebooks

Next

  • Retrain monthly on our own reviews (IMDB is a stand-in)
  • Monitor drift — alert when score distributions shift
  • Re-run the threshold sweep as costs change
  • Topic mining — why customers are unhappy, not just that they are

In one line

This is not a trained classifier. It is a system that turns text into a business decision — one that measured whether the expensive option was worth it, and let cost choose the threshold.
